chunk_summary: Probabilistic diagnosis of pediatric food allergy involves suspicion, confirmation, avoidance, and planned de-labeling to prevent iatrogenic harm and ensure nutritional adequacy.
learning_objectives: Understand the four stages of probabilistic diagnosis for pediatric food allergy.
Recognize the importance of planned de-labeling to prevent unnecessary dietary restrictions.
Learn how to document and manage elimination diets, including baseline symptoms and trial windows.
Identify strategies to prevent nutritional compromise during therapeutic elimination diets.
key_facts: Probabilistic diagnosis includes four stages: suspicion, confirmation, avoidance, and planned reintroduction.
Skipping planned de-labeling can lead to iatrogenic harm, including feeding aversion and increased re-exposure risk.
Baseline symptoms and growth should be documented before starting an elimination diet.
Elimination diets should have a defined trial window and explicit stop rules if no improvement is observed.
For nutritionally central foods (e.g., cow’s milk, egg, wheat), specify substitutes, supplementation, and follow-up within 2–4 weeks.
Every child on avoidance should have a documented reintroduction date and setting.
reference_quote: Skipping the final stage is a common source of iatrogenic harm: children remain labeled 'allergic,' diets stay unnecessarily narrow, feeding aversion can worsen, and risk may increase on re-exposure after prolonged avoidance because regular ingestion was not maintained. (Source: EAACI guidelines on the diagnosis of IgE-mediated food allergy)
document_key_topics: Stages of probabilistic diagnosis for food allergy
Planned de-labeling to prevent iatrogenic harm
Management of elimination diets in children
